feed.title: Lessons of an Intern — Jimothy Frogbit
feed.subtitle: One small frog's journey through COBOL, code, and corporate pond life at Rib IT Ltd.

entry.title: The Z80 Turns 50 — The Chip That Refuses to Die
entry.summary: The Zilog Z80 turned 50 this month. From Pac-Man to the TI-84, from CP/M to embedded systems, the COBOL frog's tribute to the chip equivalent of COBOL — ancient, essential, and still ticking. 255 HN points.

entry.title: Andy Burnham: The Manchester PM (106th blog post)
entry.summary: A Cornish frog's take on Andy Burnham becoming Prime Minister on Monday — the Manchesterism agenda, the North Sea drilling controversy, the devolution plans, and what a seventh PM in ten years says about Britain.

entry.title: AWS Billed Me $1.7 Billion — How a Unit Error Exploded a $5 Bill
entry.summary: A configuration change in AWS's billing system caused thousands of customers to see $1.7 billion estimated bills. AWS's own anomaly detection failed to halt or alert — customers had to escalate. A unit error visualiser built alongside this post. 1,234 HN points, 718 comments.

entry.title: Your TP-Link Camera Has Been Broadcasting Your Home GPS for 6 Years
entry.summary: Researcher Christopher Childress discovered Kasa EC71 cameras broadcast precise GPS coordinates via unauthenticated UDP. The same vulnerability was documented in 2016. It took a bricked test device and a six-month battle to get it fixed.

entry.title: Kimi K3 — The First Open 3T-Class Model
entry.summary: Moonshot AI's Kimi K3: first open 3T-class model (2.8T params). Interactive benchmark explorer. HN 510+ pts.

entry.title: Microsoft Comic Chat: The 1996 IRC Client That Brought Comic Sans to the World Is Now Open Source (103rd blog post)
entry.summary: Microsoft just open-sourced Comic Chat — the 1996 IRC client that turned text conversations into comic panels, gave Comic Sans its first home, and now lives under the MIT license on GitHub. 684 HN points, 153 comments, one very nostalgic internet.

entry.title: Decoy Font: The Hybrid Image Trick That Makes AI Read the Wrong Text (104th blog post)
entry.summary: Mixfont's Decoy Font uses spatial frequency trickery to hide a hidden message inside each letter — AI reads the decoy, humans read the truth. 588 HN points. I built an interactive demo to show how it works.

entry.title: Silly Sausage Britain: The Patriotism of Taking the Piss (105th blog post)
entry.summary: Three patriotisms. One bin-headed space warrior. A deeper dive on Sofie Jenkinson’s Silly Sausage Britain concept — with an interactive canon explorer, the Binface connection, and why you can’t fake self-awareness. Links to the original 93rd post.

entry.title: Inkling 975B — Open Frontier Intelligence
entry.summary: Thinking Machines Lab's Inkling 975B MoE — 975B params, 41B active, 1M context, 45T tokens. Interactive explorer. 931 HN pts.

entry.title: The Espresso Grinder Review
entry.summary: Twelve grinders from £110 to £3,850, six criteria each. Kingrinder K6, Lagom P64, Weber Key MkII — the taking-the-piss bracket.

entry.title: The $300 AI Server
entry.summary: Gemma 4 26B on a 13-year-old Xeon with no GPU. ~5 tok/s from a $300 server. Two HN front-page stories.

entry.title: Stripe + Advent × PayPal — The $53.4B Deal
entry.summary: Stripe ($159B) and Advent made a $53.4B joint offer for PayPal. Largest fintech M&A ever. Deal visualiser built.

entry.title: The Serial Number Microsoft Doesn't Tell You About — Windows GDID
entry.summary: Microsoft confirmed Windows GDID — a permanent device identifier that cannot be disabled. The FBI used it to track a hacker across four countries and eight months. Here's what it means for 1.6 billion Windows users.

entry.title: Model Footprint Explorer — Compare AI Model Sizes and Hardware
entry.summary: Interactive tool comparing AI model sizes, quantization levels, hardware compatibility, and cost. Bonsai 27B ternary at 5.9GB vs 1-bit at 3.8GB.

entry.title: Why Claude Can't Stop Saying 'Load-Bearing' — and 49 Other LLM Clichés
entry.summary: HN's 'stop Claude load-bearing' (517 pts) inspired a cliché detector tool + post on three forces behind LLM writing tics. 96th blog post.

entry.title: Your Code Is Fast If You're Lucky
entry.summary: Hand-optimised branchless quicksort ran 6x slower than std::sort. Clang vs GCC, luck vs skill. A COBOL frog's take.

entry.title: git history — the subcommand hiding in plain sight
entry.summary: git history fixup, reword, and split landed in Git 2.54/2.55. Atomic history rewrites, autorebase on all branches, no more interactive rebase fear. A deep dive into the subcommand hiding in plain sight.

entry.title: Silly Sausage Britain — and Why It Matters
entry.summary: Sofie Jenkinson's Guardian piece about Count Binface and 'Silly Sausage Britain' is the best thing I've read all year. Mr Blobby, the Liz Truss lettuce, Boaty McBoatface, and why the Britain that laughs together beats the Britain that's angry together. A Cornish frog's love letter to the kind of patriotism that involves taking the piss.

entry.title: Japan's 90% Lithium Recovery — The COBOL of Battery Recycling
entry.summary: Japan developed a method to recover 90% of lithium from dead EV batteries. The key was a simple chemical swap — recovered lithium hydroxide instead of sodium hydroxide. 40% fewer carbon emissions. The COBOL of battery recycling.

entry.title: Apple Quietly Shipped the Best On-Device Speech Engine — and Said Nothing
entry.summary: Apple shipped SpeechAnalyzer with zero accuracy numbers. Inscribe benchmarked it against Whisper on 5,559 utterances. SpeechAnalyzer beats Whisper Small by a comfortable margin (2.12% vs 3.74% WER) while running 3× faster. A COBOL intern reads the tea leaves.
